steps.title: Sine & Cosine as Coordinates
steps.goal: See that the terminal point's coordinates are (cos θ, sin θ).
steps.introText: On the **unit circle** (radius 1), the terminal point of angle θ has coordinates **(cos θ, sin θ)**. The horizontal leg is cos θ and the vertical leg is sin θ.
steps.prompt: Drag the point and watch the red (cosine) and green (sine) legs. The coordinates you read off are exactly cos θ and sin θ.

steps.title: Special-Angle Coordinates
steps.goal: Read the exact coordinates at common angles.
steps.introText: At the special angles the coordinates are exact values like **±1/2**, **±√2/2**, and **±√3/2**. Memorizing a few lets you find the rest using symmetry.
steps.prompt: Snap to at least four different special angles and note how the signs of cos θ and sin θ change between quadrants.

steps.title: Coordinates at π/3
steps.prompt: Drag the point to θ = π/3 (60°), where (cos θ, sin θ) = (1/2, √3/2).

feedback.correct: Perfect! At 60° the point is (1/2, √3/2) — short cosine, tall sine.
feedback.incorrect: Not yet — π/3 is 60°, where cosine is 1/2 and sine is √3/2.
feedback.hint: π/3 = 60°, in Quadrant I. The x-coordinate should read 0.5.

steps.title: Negative Cosine
steps.goal: Use the signs of coordinates to find a quadrant-II point.
steps.info: In Quadrant II, x is negative and y is positive, so **cos θ < 0** while **sin θ > 0**.
steps.prompt: Place the point where cos θ = −√2/2 and sin θ = √2/2 (that's θ = 3π/4).

feedback.incorrect: Check the signs — you need a negative x and a positive y, both with size √2/2.
feedback.hint: √2/2 ≈ 0.71. You want the point in Quadrant II, at 135° (3π/4).

steps.title: Where Sine Goes Negative
steps.goal: Reason about the sign of sine.
steps.prompt: Place the point so that sin θ is negative but cos θ is positive.

feedback.correct: Exactly! Quadrant IV is where cosine is positive and sine is negative.
feedback.incorrect: You need the lower-right region: positive x (cos), negative y (sin).
feedback.hint: Sine is the y-coordinate. For y < 0 and x > 0, head to Quadrant IV (270°–360°).

steps.title: Signs Around the Circle
steps.goal: Summarize the sign pattern of sine and cosine.
steps.prompt: In which quadrant are both sin θ and cos θ negative?
steps.feedback: Right! In Quadrant III both coordinates are negative, so sine and cosine are both negative.
steps.incorrectFeedback: Remember cos θ is the x-coordinate and sin θ is the y-coordinate. Both are negative only in the lower-left quadrant.

steps.title: Mastery Check
steps.goal: Find four points on the unit circle — no hints.

unitCircleSequence.label: Any point with sin θ < 0 and cos θ < 0

steps.completionMessage: Well done! You can read sine and cosine straight from the unit circle. Next, we'll unroll the circle into the sine and cosine graphs.
